flavor_profile.texture: Smooth, fiberless flesh in premium varieties; julienned crisp when green

how_to_eat: Peel and slice fresh for eating
Eat green with nam pla wan (sweet-salty fish sauce dip)
Blend into smoothies or mango shakes
Serve with sticky rice and coconut cream (khao niao mamuang)
Slice into wedges and refrigerate before eating

where_to_buy.markets: All fresh markets, talad rot fai, morning markets
where_to_buy.price_range_baht: 20–80 per kg depending on variety and season
where_to_buy.notes: Nam Dok Mai and Mahachanok are premium eating varieties; Khieo Sawoei preferred green

traveler_tips: Green mango is intentionally eaten unripe with spicy dips — don't wait for it to ripen. Mango sticky rice is best from street carts in March–May.

flavor_profile.texture: Soft, juicy white segments; melts on the tongue

how_to_eat: Score the purple rind around the equator and twist apart — do not cut through
Pull off the top half of rind to reveal white segments
Eat the white flesh segments directly; avoid the purple rind (bitter)
The segment count matches the petal count on the base — a Thai trick
Refrigerate for 15 minutes before eating for best flavor

where_to_buy.markets: Seasonal markets, fresh markets in southern Thailand
where_to_buy.notes: Called the 'Queen of Fruits' in Thailand. Dark purple rind should give slightly when pressed.

traveler_tips: The purple rind stains clothing permanently. Eat over a napkin. Buy only when rind gives slightly — hard means unripe, very soft means overripe.

flavor_profile.texture: Translucent, slippery, juicy flesh around a central seed

how_to_eat: Pinch and score the hairy skin with your thumbnail along the equator
Twist apart the two halves of the skin
Pop the flesh into your mouth and spit out the seed
Eat fresh; also good chilled

where_to_buy.markets: All fresh markets, especially in southern provinces
where_to_buy.notes: Look for bright red specimens with green-tipped spines — freshest indicator

traveler_tips: The word 'rambutan' means 'hairy' in Malay. The soft spines are not sharp. Red with green tips = fresh; all-brown spines = old.

flavor_profile.texture: Creamy, dense, almost custard or ice cream texture; some varieties stringy

how_to_eat: Ask the market vendor to open it — the spiky shell requires tools
Pull the flesh pods from the interior sections
Eat the yellow or cream-colored flesh pods directly
Avoid eating with alcohol — Thai belief and some scientific basis
Try Monthong for mildest entry point; Kan Yao for premium experience

where_to_buy.markets: Dedicated durian stalls, fresh markets, roadside vendors in season
where_to_buy.notes: Monthong is milder and more accessible. Kan Yao is the premium small-seed variety. Banned in many hotels and transport.

traveler_tips: Banned in many hotels, airports, and public transport due to smell. Buy from a vendor who will open it on-site. The smell is intense outside; the taste inside is completely different — rich, sweet, complex.

flavor_profile.texture: Juicy, slippery translucent flesh; refreshing

seasonality.north_thailand: Jul–Sep peak, Lamphun is the longan capital

how_to_eat: Pinch the thin brown shell and pop the flesh out whole
Eat fresh; spit the smooth brown seed
Sold in clusters — peel and eat one at a time
Excellent dried as a snack or in herbal drinks

where_to_buy.markets: All markets in season, especially Lamphun and Chiang Mai area
where_to_buy.notes: Lamphun province is famous for its longan festival in August

traveler_tips: Longan is considered cooling in Thai traditional medicine. The Lamphun Longan Festival (August) is a great cultural event if you're in the north.

flavor_profile.texture: Juicy, tender, slightly fibrous; very refreshing

seasonality.north_thailand: Apr–Jun, Chiang Rai and Chiang Mai

how_to_eat: Peel the rough red skin by pinching and pulling
Eat the white flesh whole, spitting the smooth seed
Chill before eating for best flavor
Use in cocktails, desserts, or eat fresh by the handful

where_to_buy.notes: Chiang Rai and Chiang Mai produce the best Thai lychees. Hong Huay is the premium variety.

traveler_tips: Fresh lychee is very different from canned. If you've only had canned, fresh is a revelation. Buy clusters with red-pink skin, not brown.

flavor_profile.texture: Soft, smooth, melting; green papaya is firm and crunchy

how_to_eat: Cut in half lengthwise and scoop out the seeds with a spoon
Slice or cube the orange flesh and eat fresh
Squeeze lime juice over ripe papaya to brighten the flavor
Eat green papaya shredded in som tum (papaya salad)

where_to_buy.notes: Green papaya for som tum is cheap and abundant. Ripe papaya should yield slightly to pressure.

traveler_tips: Som tum (green papaya salad) is one of Thailand's most iconic dishes. The unripe green fruit is used for cooking, not the ripe orange one.

flavor_profile.texture: Juicy, fibrous, refreshing; Thai varieties often much sweeter than imports

how_to_eat: Street vendors sell pre-cut in plastic bags with a salt-chili shaker on the side
Sprinkle the salt-sugar-chili mix for the authentic Thai way
Peel, core, and cut into rings or wedges at home
Use in fried rice (khao pad sapparot) or as a palate cleanser

where_to_buy.price_range_baht: 20–50 per prepared bag, 30–80 per whole fruit
where_to_buy.notes: Pattani and Prachuap Khiri Khan are famous pineapple regions. Thai varieties (Pattavia, Trad Si Thong) are much sweeter than western varieties.

traveler_tips: The Thai street food trick: pineapple + salt + chili flakes. The salt intensifies sweetness and the chili adds heat. Try it before judging.

flavor_profile.texture: Soft, speckled with tiny black seeds throughout; smooth and cooling

how_to_eat: Cut in half lengthwise
Scoop out the flesh with a spoon, or score and peel back the skin
Cut into cubes for fruit platters
Blend into smoothies — adds color and mild sweetness

where_to_buy.markets: All supermarkets and most fresh markets
where_to_buy.notes: Red-fleshed varieties are sweeter and more flavorful. White-fleshed is milder and more common.

traveler_tips: Dragon fruit has mild flavor — don't expect explosive taste. Its value is visual appeal, hydration, and fiber. The red-fleshed variety is noticeably sweeter.

flavor_profile.texture: Chewy, fibrous pods; the interior seeds are also edible when boiled

how_to_eat: Vendors typically pre-cut and package the pods — buy these
Pull the yellow pods free from the stringy white core
The seeds inside each pod are edible when boiled or roasted
Young green jackfruit is used in savory curries and stir-fries

where_to_buy.markets: Fresh markets, usually pre-cut and packaged
where_to_buy.notes: Full jackfruits can weigh 10–25kg — you'll almost always buy pre-cut. The fresh cut variety is much better than packaged.

traveler_tips: Jackfruit is the world's largest tree fruit. The sticky latex sap stains hands and clothing — oil your hands before handling or just buy pre-cut. Young green jackfruit is a popular meat substitute.

flavor_profile.texture: Large, dry-textured segments; less juicy than grapefruit but sweeter

seasonality.north_thailand: Sep–Jan, Nakhon Pathom is famous for pomelo

how_to_eat: Score the thick outer skin in quarters with a knife
Peel back the four sections of thick rind
Separate the fruit segments and remove the bitter white membrane
Eat fresh segments, use in yam som-o (pomelo salad)

where_to_buy.notes: Nakhon Pathom Thong Dee variety is considered the best in Thailand. Choose heavy fruits with thin skin.

traveler_tips: Pomelo is milder and sweeter than grapefruit. The thick pith can be used to make candied pomelo peel — a popular Thai sweet.

flavor_profile.texture: Crisp, watery, crunchy; like an apple-watermelon cross

how_to_eat: Rinse and eat whole like an apple — skin is edible
Core the small seed cluster at the bottom if preferred
Dip in plum powder or salt-sugar mix, as Thai children do
Slice for fruit platters

where_to_buy.notes: Bell-shaped, waxy pink or green skin. Thai name chomphu means pink/rose in Thai.

traveler_tips: Rose apple is mostly water and refreshing in hot weather. Perfect for the 38°C Chiang Mai summer afternoons. Try the dark pink variety — sweeter.

flavor_profile.texture: Sticky, paste-like flesh clinging to hard seeds; fibrous veins

how_to_eat: Crack the brittle brown pod and peel off the shell
Pull away the fibrous veins running along the sticky flesh
Eat the tangy flesh directly, spitting the seeds
Sweet tamarind candy (makham wan) is sold pre-packaged
Used extensively in pad thai, sauces, and curries

where_to_buy.markets: All fresh markets in season, dried/paste year-round
where_to_buy.notes: Makham wan (sweet variety) is a common snack. Makham piek (sour) is used in cooking.

traveler_tips: Tamarind is the backbone of pad thai and many Thai sauces. The sweet candy variety (makham wan) is a great first taste — sweeter and less intense than the sour cooking variety.

flavor_profile.texture: Crisp, watery, refreshing; the thin skin is edible

how_to_eat: Slice crosswise to reveal the iconic star shape
Eat with skin on — the skin is edible
Sprinkle with salt, sugar, and chili powder
Use as garnish for drinks and platters
Good in fruit salads

where_to_buy.notes: Sweet varieties are greener-yellow when ripe; sour varieties used for cooking. Trim the five ridges if they look brown.

traveler_tips: The star cross-section makes starfruit a favorite for photos. Yellow-orange color means sweet; pale green means more tart. Avoid if you have kidney disease (contains oxalic acid).

flavor_profile.texture: Spongy, cotton-like outer flesh; inner translucent segments around seeds

how_to_eat: Score around the equator and twist to open
Suck the sweet-sour juice from the spongy outer flesh
The inner translucent segments around the seeds are the best part
Seeds are not eaten; the flesh attached to seeds requires sucking off
Sold with sugar or chili-salt dips at markets

where_to_buy.notes: Look for yellow-orange skin. Green means unripe.

traveler_tips: Santol requires patience — the technique involves sucking the flesh. It's a Thai childhood snack. Messy but rewarding. Don't bite into the seed.

flavor_profile.texture: Crunchy outer flesh; soft seedy core; Thai varieties often very crunchy

how_to_eat: Rinse and eat whole like an apple, or slice into wedges
Thai style: eat with a small bag of plum powder + sugar + chili dip
The core is seedy — avoid or eat around it
Juice well for fresh guava juice

where_to_buy.notes: Farang also means foreigner in Thai — a joke about the fruit being as common as tourists. Look for firm, pale-green fruits.

traveler_tips: The word 'farang' means both guava and foreigner in Thai. Thai-style guava is eaten firm and crunchy — not soft and ripe like Western preference. The plum powder dip is essential.

flavor_profile.texture: Dry, dense, apple-like crunch; astringent skin must be removed

how_to_eat: Pinch the tip and peel back the scaly brown skin
The lobes of pale yellow flesh separate easily
Each lobe contains a seed — don't bite through
Best eaten fresh; can be very astringent if overripe

where_to_buy.notes: Scaly brown skin resembles snake scales, hence 'snake fruit'. Squeeze to check: hard = good; soft = overripe.

traveler_tips: The scaly skin looks intimidating but peels easily. The flesh has a unique dry texture unlike any other fruit. The astringent tannin finish is an acquired taste.

flavor_profile.texture: Translucent, juicy, grape-like flesh; thin segments around small seeds

how_to_eat: Squeeze the thin skin and pop the flesh out
Eat the translucent segments; spit the seed if bitter
Best eaten cold
The skin produces a sap when cut — wash hands

where_to_buy.notes: Surat Thani province produces excellent langsat. Related to longkong (slightly different variety, less sap in skin).

traveler_tips: Langsat and longkong are similar fruits often confused. Longkong has less bitter skin sap. Both are delicious eaten fresh in clusters.

flavor_profile.texture: Soft and creamy when ripe; firm and starchy when green; tiny varieties more aromatic

how_to_eat: Peel and eat fresh — Kluay Khai (egg banana) is the best for fresh eating
Deep fry battered (kluay tod) for a crispy street snack
Grill in skin over charcoal for kluay ping
Use in Thai desserts with coconut milk (kluay buat chi)

where_to_buy.markets: Every market and street corner year-round
where_to_buy.notes: Thailand has 20+ banana varieties. Kluay Khai (egg banana, finger-sized) is the sweetest. Kluay Nam Wa is for cooking and frying. Ignore the common Cavendish — seek local varieties.

traveler_tips: Don't settle for Cavendish. Thai markets sell 5–8 local varieties. Kluay Khai is tiny and intensely sweet. Kluay Hom is the most fragrant. Fried banana on a stick (kluay tod) is a perfect street snack.

flavor_profile.texture: Young coconut: thin, jelly-soft white flesh; mature: hard, dense white flesh

how_to_eat: Young coconut: ask vendor to chop the top — drink the water with a straw
After drinking, ask vendor to split it — scoop the soft jelly flesh with a spoon
Mature coconut: crack the hard shell, use the white flesh in cooking
Coconut milk pressed from mature flesh is used in almost all Thai curries and desserts

where_to_buy.markets: Every fresh market and street food stall
where_to_buy.notes: Young coconuts (maprao on) are sold pre-trimmed as drink vessels. Mature coconuts (maprao haeng) are for cooking.

traveler_tips: In 35°C heat, a fresh young coconut from a street vendor is transformative. Cold coconut water rehydrates better than sports drinks. The vendor will cut a spoon from the trimmed husk — use it to scrape the jelly flesh.

flavor_profile.texture: Grainy, pear-like; slightly gritty texture; very sweet and dense

how_to_eat: Peel the thin brown skin with a knife or by hand when very ripe
Eat the brown flesh directly — very sweet
Remove the few glossy black seeds (they have a hook — be careful)
Best eaten fully ripe when it yields to gentle pressure

where_to_buy.notes: Looks like a potato or kiwi on the outside. Ripe when slightly soft. Immature sapodilla contains bitter latex.

traveler_tips: This underrated fruit tastes like caramel and brown sugar. The seeds have a small hook on one end — do not swallow. Fully ripe lamut is one of the sweetest fruits in Thailand.

flavor_profile.texture: Creamy, custard-like; individual segments pull away from the core; grainy undertone

how_to_eat: Press gently — it should yield softly when ripe
Pull apart with fingers into two halves
Eat the white custard segments by hand
Each segment has one inedible black seed — spit it out
Eat over a bowl — it can be very messy

where_to_buy.notes: The bumpy green skin should separate when ripe. Very perishable — eat within a day of buying ripe fruit.

traveler_tips: Custard apple is genuinely one of the most remarkably sweet Thai fruits. Eat it the day you buy it. Every segment has a seed — don't accidentally swallow. The flavor is like vanilla custard.

flavor_profile.texture: Juicy jewel-like arils; each contains a small edible seed; refreshing burst

how_to_eat: Score the skin into sections and pull apart over a bowl of water
The arils sink; white pith floats — makes separation easy
Eat the ruby arils as-is, or juice them
Sprinkle on salads, desserts, or yogurt
Fresh-squeezed pomegranate juice sold at markets is excellent

where_to_buy.notes: Thai pomegranates tend to be sweeter than Middle Eastern varieties. Fresh juice stands are common.

traveler_tips: Thai name thabthim also refers to the jewel in certain Thai desserts (red rubies in coconut milk). Fresh-pressed pomegranate juice at market juice stalls is intensely good.

flavor_profile.texture: Very juicy, watery, crisp red or yellow flesh; cooling effect

how_to_eat: Cut into wedges and eat cold
Thai vendors sell pre-cut slices from carts
Blend with lime for a watermelon-lime slushie
Sprinkle with salt to enhance sweetness (Thai technique)

where_to_buy.markets: Every market and street vendor year-round
where_to_buy.notes: Thai watermelons are small to medium and very sweet. Seedless and black-seeded varieties available.

traveler_tips: Thailand's heat makes cold watermelon essential survival equipment. The bright red pre-cut slices on market carts cost almost nothing. Yellow-flesh variety (taeng mo lueang) is even sweeter.

flavor_profile.texture: Crisp, dense, apple-like crunch; firm flesh with no give

how_to_eat: Peel and julienne or slice into sticks
Eat with nam pla wan dip (sweet fish sauce with dried shrimp)
Eat with prik kluea (chili-salt mix) for simpler snacking
Use shredded in som tum (green mango version)
Eaten as a snack throughout the day

where_to_buy.markets: All fresh markets in mango season
where_to_buy.price_range_baht: 10–30 per bag of sliced pieces with dip
where_to_buy.notes: Intentionally eaten unripe and sour. The dip (nam pla wan) is sweet-salty-savory — essential pairing. Khieo Sawoei variety is the most popular for eating green.

traveler_tips: This is one of the most confusing Thai food experiences for travelers — the mango is intentionally under-ripe and sour. Do not wait for it to turn yellow. The nam pla wan dip transforms the sourness into something addictive.
